title: Text Augmentation
concept: Text augmentation creates alternate phrasings such as synonym swaps, back-translation, or paraphrases so language models learn the underlying meaning instead of one exact wording.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is image augmentation trying to teach a vision model?

options.text: That small visual changes do not have to change the prediction.
That every object should get a new label after a rotation.
That color is the only useful feature in photos.

exercises.explain: Correct. Image augmentation teaches stability across harmless visual variation.

cards.title: One idea can be said many ways
cards.body: A customer can say “this app is great,” “I really like this app,” or “this app works beautifully.” If a language model only trusts one exact wording, it misses the bigger pattern: the sentiment is still positive.

cards.title: How text augmentation works
cards.body: Text augmentation creates alternate versions of a sentence using tools like synonym replacement, back-translation, or paraphrasing. The point is to keep the core meaning while varying the surface wording.

cards.title: Same play, different actors
cards.body: A play is still the same story when different actors speak the lines with slightly different phrasing or rhythm. Text augmentation aims for that same effect: new wording, same message.

cards.title: Back-translation as a rewrite tool
cards.body: A sentence like “The package arrived late” might be translated into another language and then translated back into English. The final wording may change, but the complaint usually stays the same. That gives the model a second phrasing of the same idea.

cards.title: Text meaning is fragile
cards.body: A common myth is that any synonym swap is safe. But some words change tone, strength, or even meaning. Text augmentation needs extra care because language is subtle, and a small wording change can quietly change the label.

cards.title: Exact wording vs. preserved meaning
cards.body: Strong text augmentation protects the meaning while changing the phrasing.

left.text:
The model trusts one favorite phrase.
New phrasing may confuse it.

right.text:
Several phrasings carry the same idea.
The model becomes less brittle.

exercises.prompt: Why is text augmentation often trickier than image augmentation?

options.text: Because a small wording change can quietly change the meaning or sentiment.
Because text models cannot use training data.
Because words have no patterns at all.
Because sentences can never be paraphrased.

exercises.explain: Exactly. Language is subtle, so “small change” does not always mean “same label.”

exercises.prompt: True or false: Back-translation is useful because it can produce a new phrasing while keeping the same core idea.
exercises.explain: True. It is a common way to get alternate wording without starting from a blank page.

exercises.sentence: Useful text augmentation should preserve the original ___.
exercises.explain: Meaning is correct. If the meaning changes, the old label may no longer fit.

exercises.prompt: Which option sounds like careful text augmentation for a sentiment model?

options.text: Turn “I loved the movie” into “I really enjoyed the film” and keep the positive label.
Turn “I loved the movie” into “I hated the movie” and keep the positive label.
Delete half the sentence at random even if the sentiment word disappears.

exercises.explain: Right. The paraphrase changes the wording but not the sentiment.

exercises.prompt: A review classifier only reacts strongly to the phrase “highly recommend” and misses other positive wording like “worth every penny” or “I would buy it again.” What is the best augmentation idea?

options.text: Add meaning-preserving paraphrases, synonym-based rewrites, or back-translated versions so the model learns positive intent beyond one favorite phrase.
Keep training on the same exact phrase until the model forgets all other wording.
Change positive reviews into negative ones but keep their old labels.
Remove every uncommon phrase from the dataset so wording never varies.

exercises.explain: Exactly. The model needs more positive wording patterns so it stops tying sentiment to one exact sentence shape.
